data.title: Why Nokia Fell After Posting Q3 2024 Earnings (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In the past four months, Nokia Oyj (NYSE:NOK) gained around 13%, beating the S&P 500’s (SP500) return of 3.6%. NOK stock broke out below $4.00 after reporting second-quarter results.

For the third quarter, the network infrastructure firm posted revenue falling from the previous year. In addition, Nokia did not raise its outlook. Following that, NOK stock traded down by around 5% in pre-market trade.

Is the reaction justified? The stock ran up before the report and risks giving back some of that gain.

Nokia Q3 2024 Results

In the third quarter, Nokia reported earnings of EUR 0.06 (US $0.065) per share. Revenue fell by 8.1% year-on-year to EUR 4.33 billion (US $4.7 billion). Chief Executive Officer Pekka Lundmark said in the earnings press release that he is optimistic that the company is turning the corner in many parts of its business.

Net sales in Network Infrastructure grew in the quarter. Order backlog grew in the period. Fixed Networks grew by 9%, while IP Networks revenue grew by 6%. Overall, the Network Infrastructure net sales fell by 1.0% Y/Y. Gross margin increased to 42.1%, compared to 40.5% last year. This indicated that the company is not chasing low-profit or money-losing contracts.

Still, looking ahead, the firm issued a negative net sales growth forecast for the network segment. It expects negative growth of up to -6.0%. Despite falling revenue, this unit accounts for the highest operating margin in the double-digit percentage.

The CEO highlighted the excellent momentum in 5G Core, suggesting potential growth from network automation, cloudification, and enabling network APIs. It will capture opportunities by expanding its presence in the data center market. To achieve that goal, it will invest to broaden its product portfolio in IP Networks.

The business from Nokia Technologies continued to stabilize. The firm concluded its smartphone renewal cycle. It will seek business expansion into new growth areas.

Opportunities

Nokia proposed an acquisition of Infinera (INFN) for $2.3 billion on June 27, 2024. This will likely close after the HSR waiting period for the Justice Department’s review has expired. The up to $93 million funding from the CHIPS Act is a positive tailwind for Infinera. The combination of investment tax credits and direct funding could total over $200 million in federal incentives.

Traders may view the impact of CHIPS Act funding on a stock. Wolfspeed (WOLF) received $1.5 billion in total funding. It will get up to $750 million from the CHIPS Act and $750 million from a consortium of investment funds led by Apollo (APO).

I recently suggested in this tax-loss selling article that Wolfspeed is a stock to hold.

Shareholder Returns

On October 17, the Board of Directors resolved to pay out a dividend of EUR 0.03 a share to shareholders of record on October 22. To return up to EUR 600 million, Nokia declared a buyback program in January 2024. On July 19, it accelerated the timeframe of the buyback program. It will complete the EUR 600 million buyback by the end of this year, compared to the original two-year timeline.

The company is paying for Infinera with up to 30% in stock. To offset dilution, The Board committed to buy back more stock. This is on top of the current EUR 600 million plan. So far, the company bought back 84.29 million shares at an average price of EUR 3.48 (US $3.77). In comparison, NOK stock closed at $4.46 on Oct. 16. At the time of writing, shares will trade at around $4.20.

Risks

Q3 net sales fell mostly from 75% of the decline driven by India. Nokia reported stronger results in the year-ago period, hurting the comparable results. Despite risks of wider negative revenue growth, gross margins are expanding. Nokia improved its product and regional mix. It also cut product costs. The continued drive for higher efficiency and lowering operating expenses will raise its profitability.

Expect Nokia to manage the continued intense competition without sacrificing profitability. It will win contracts by remaining disciplined on price. The more it reduces its costs, the better it may compete on lower product pricing.

NOK Stock Grade

Income investors should consider Nokia’s weak dividend grades. While the stock yields 3.13%, the stock has weak dividend safety, growth, and consistency. Despite the Infinera acquisition, the dividend safety and growth grades improved from three months ago:

Nokia’s valuation is compelling. It scored an A- on that factor grade:

Looking closely at its growth, negative revenue growth is hurting its growth grade.

Growth is negative on every metric, compared to the positive figures from the sector median. The operating cash flow growth, however, is a bright spot. Nokia is cutting costs and growing profit margins. Operating FCF grew threefold, compared to the average of 17.47%.

According to Stock Rover, Nokia shares have a fair value of $4.59. This is based on the margin of safety, which is a difference between a company's fair value and its price.

In comparison, Wall Street has a $4.92 target price on NOK stock.

Your Takeaway

Nokia’s post-earnings decline is justified. The stock may trade at more than 6% lower as markets proverbially “sell the stock on the news.” In the near term, Nokia may trade back to its historical range of $3.75 — $4.25.

To break out above $4.00, Nokia needs to report revenue growth in all of its sectors. The gross margin improvement may not last after the company cannot find more cost cuts. Competitors will pressure Nokia’s product prices. As a result, net sales from Network Infrastructure need to increase in the next year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fiduciary Management Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Outlook
data.description:
Domestic and international equity markets rose in the September quarter, capping off an exceptional run over the past 12 months. With gains of 36.35% in the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) , 26.76% in the Russell 2000 Index (RTY), and 24.77% in the MSCI EAFE Index ('USD'), the indices far outpaced historical average returns. Inflation has moderated and several central banks have started easing, with the Federal Reserve (Fed), European Central Bank (ECB), and Bank of England (BoE) each cutting interest rates. Investors celebrated the accommodations, pushing already stretched valuations to new heights.

As we take a step back, we are struggling to reconcile the stock market euphoria with much of the fundamental backdrop. That said, there are always going to be relative and absolute values in the market, and it is our job to find these regardless of the environment. We welcome volatility in the days, weeks, and months ahead, as it creates compelling long-term opportunities for the portfolios. While there are certainly pockets of strength in the global economy, such as those entities with exposure to mega cap tech (artificial intelligence/AI), obesity drugs, infrastructure, and defense spending (among the top-performing themes in the U.S. and Europe in 2024), there is also evidence building on the negative side of the ledger.

In the U.S., the unemployment rate has risen to 4.2% (from 3.7% in January), and GDP growth is expected to slow to 1.6% in 2025 (from 2.6% in 2024), according to OECD forecasts. Housing, manufacturing, transportation, technology (outside of AI capital expenditures) and discretionary spending have weakened. The low-income consumer is under significant pressure, as several years of steep inflation have taken a toll. Excess savings have been depleted and credit delinquencies, while still low by historical standards, are on the rise. Overseas, the economic backdrop is also softening. The ECB recently cut their growth forecasts for each of the next three years, with subpar household consumption and a slump in manufacturing weighing on demand. In Japan, growth is expected to shrink in 2024 before recovering modestly. China launched several stimulus programs to try to reinvigorate growth that has slowed.

Meanwhile, geopolitical risks remain high, with an expanding conflict in the Middle East, no end in sight for the Russia/Ukraine war, and aggressive Chinese posturing toward Taiwan. By the end of 2024, it will have been an election year for half of the world’s GDP, creating further consternation. In the interim, government deficits are ballooning, and debt levels are hitting new all-time highs. As shown in the chart above, global debt reached $315 trillion this year, up almost $100 trillion since 2016.

Unfortunately, equity markets do not appear to be accounting for global risks or external shocks. Valuations are seemingly priced for perfection, as earnings growth has not kept up with stock prices, leaving little margin for error. We have highlighted the S&P 500 characteristics in several prior letters, which in essence has become a concentrated growth index driven by a handful of anointed companies. Many S&P 500 multiples are in the 10th decile (most expensive) of historical valuation, with several metrics virtually unrecognizable from their long-term norms (see below):

Source: The Leuthold Group

Has U.S. economic growth justified this optimism? Will it in the future? U.S. GDP growth has been below average since the Great Financial Crisis, and with high debt burdens and difficult demographics, projected growth would not seem to warrant near all-time high valuations.

Not surprisingly, growth has outperformed value across the board in the current fiscal year, with the gap most acute in U.S. Large Cap, where the Russell 1000 Growth Index gained 42.19% versus 27.76% for the Value. As can be seen in the 50-year chart below, growth has been on a tear since 2007, after lagging by a significant margin from 1974-2007. It’s important to remember that markets can move in very long cycles, but trees (and growth stocks) do not grow to the sky. We are confident value investing will have a strong resurgence in the fullness of time, as valuation and risk aversion will eventually return to the forefront.

Despite nosebleed valuations, investors continue to pile into the U.S. stock market, chasing performance. Greed and a fear of missing out will likely lead to steady buying all the way to the eventual tipping point. As detailed in the August 2nd Grant’s Interest Rate Observer, “Inflows into passive growth funds in 2024 [are] on pace to double vs. 2023, which was already the highest year on record.” Per J.P. Morgan, stock allocations in U.S. households recently accounted for 42% of their total financial assets, the most on record dating back to 1952. Risk appetites appear to be showing no signs of abating.

As the market seemingly celebrates new daily record highs, it feeds off a never-ending obsession with the Fed’s every move. It’s widely accepted that rate cuts are good news for the economy and stock market. We have a different perspective. As we wrote in our September 2022 letter: “Did the move from ‘normal’ interest rates prior to the Great Financial Crisis to zero percent rates for years afterwards cause the economy to boom? No! Economic growth was weak over the ensuing 15 years. Abnormally low rates boosted asset prices and set the mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and private equity markets aflame, but it did very little for organic economic growth. We had a boom in financial engineering.” We do not believe you can cut your way to prosperity.

There Are Still Values to be Had

Our long-time readers know that FMI has typically lagged in more speculative-driven bull markets (similar to today’s) and outperformed in more challenging environments, a direct result of our valuation framework, focus on business quality, and balance sheet strength. Many investors today have never seen an extended bear market, and repeatedly have been rewarded for buying the dip. At some point this dynamic will change, as will risk tolerance. In the meantime, we will remain cautious, trying to find the best available values amidst the opportunity set.

While valuations are elevated across market caps and geographies, fortunately it’s less extreme outside of U.S. mega cap technology. Compared with the Magnificent Seven (Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Tesla, and Meta Platforms), the other 493 stocks in the S&P 500 trade at ~31% lower valuations, using a basket of metrics. The FMI Large Cap strategy trades at a ~30% and ~50% discount to the 493 and Magnificent Seven, respectively, for what we believe are well-run, above-average businesses1.

For comparison, profitable small cap companies in the U.S. trade near their widest discount to large caps in over 40 years, aside from the 2000 tech bubble. In recent years, incredible gains and concentration in the technology sector have been a key large cap driver. With the Nasdaq Composite index (COMP:IND, a proxy for technology) gaining 44.70% in 2023 and 21.84% in the first nine months of 2024, small cap companies (and nearly everything else) have failed to keep pace. As illustrated in the chart below, make no mistake that this dynamic can flip in a hurry, something tech investors from 2000 would like to – and seem to currently – forget.

The FMI Small Cap strategy has had a very strong run in recent years but gave up some modest ground in the most recent quarter, as money-losing companies and financials (lower quality and/or highly levered) drove the Russell 2000. We estimate that the FMI Small Cap strategy trades at a ~37% discount to the Russell 20001. We feel great about our relative positioning when compared to the subpar business quality throughout the benchmark.

While still high from a historical perspective, overseas stock valuations are not nearly as egregious as their American counterparts. Many comparable companies in Europe and Asia trade at meaningful discounts to U.S. peers. The table below illustrates this dynamic, with a collection of international companies trading at average and median discounts of 31% and 44%, respectively on an EV/Sales basis.

Last quarter, we questioned whether the Japanese market had gotten a bit ahead of itself. On cue, the Nikkei 225 Index (NKY:IND) came under pressure in the September quarter, and much of the headwind we faced earlier in the year from our significant underweight in Japan has abated. The FMI International strategy continues to trade at a discount to the MSCI EAFE Index.

With heightened risks and demanding valuations, prudence and patience are warranted. Our clients can take comfort that FMI has a long history of downside protection in more difficult markets. We expect that our discipline will pay dividends when those who have been less discerning and chasing returns ultimately face the music, which is playing louder with each passing quarter.

Listed below are a few portfolio holdings where we see attractive absolute and relative values:

Arrow Electronics Inc. (ARW) – Small Cap/All Cap

Arrow is a distributor of electronic components and enterprise computing solutions. As a distributor, the company benefits from global technology growth without being directly exposed to technology inventor’s risk. Arrow has a diversified set of vendors, customers, and end-markets, as the company serves over 100,000 customers in 85 countries, including thousands of value-added resellers (VARs). No single customer accounts for more than 2% of sales, and their biggest supplier accounts for 10% of sales. The company has performed well over the long-term, growing sales while earning a return on invested capital ('ROIC') that exceeds its cost of capital. Today, Arrow trades near its historical P/E multiple, yet earnings are depressed due to lower technology spending. Additionally, the company trades at a little over book value, which is below its historical average. While there is some cyclicality in Arrow’s end markets, we like the counter-cyclical free cash flow profile of the business combined with management’s capital allocation priorities. The company has been advantageously repurchasing its stock, helping earnings per share to increase at attractive rates that are much higher than operating profits. Over the past five years, shares outstanding have decreased significantly, with EPS compounding in the teens. The valuation multiples are attractive given the business quality and growth prospects.

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) – Large Cap/All Cap/Global/ Focused Global

Berkshire Hathaway is a diversified holding company that owns subsidiaries in insurance and reinsurance, freight rail transportation, utilities, energy, manufacturing, services, retail, and finance. Chairman, controlling shareholder, and famed investor Warren Buffett uses the permanent capital base of the large insurance assets – along with disciplined underwriting standards – to drive value-added investments that compound shareholder returns over time. From its inception in 1965 through 2023, Berkshire’s market value has increased at an annual growth rate of 19.8% vs. 10.2% for the S&P 500. Berkshire’s primary assets are its large insurance businesses, including GEICO, General Re, and National Indemnity, which we estimate at ~35% of the company’s value. The enduring competitive advantages of Berkshire’s insurance assets have allowed it to increase its float at a higher rate than its peers and provide low-cost funding for a strong collection of operating businesses, including BNSF Railroad, McLane Foodservice, Pilot Travel, Marmon Industries, Dairy Queen, and many others. On conservative estimates, we believe the company is trading at over a 20% discount on a sum- of-the-parts basis, and carries a mid-teens earnings multiple when factoring in a healthy net cash position and look-through EPS from its public equity portfolio. In an expensive market, we find the valuation to be significantly more appealing than the S&P 500. We expect a combination of strong operations and value- added acquisitions to increase the intrinsic value of the company at an above-average rate going forward.

Informa PLC (OTCPK:IFPJF) – International

Informa is a leader in corporate events and operates an attractive core business in organizing and operating industry trade shows across the globe. The firm has grown through both organic and inorganic show development, building defensible positions serving attractive industry niches. Informa’s competitive advantages stem from the network effects inherent to its flagship offerings, scale advantages in procurement, and venue access that the firm enjoys over smaller rivals. As the events business continues to normalize post-pandemic, we expect a period of above-normal revenue growth and margin expansion to persist over the coming years. On a normalized basis, the firm’s financial profile is characterized by mid-single-digit revenue growth and low- double-digit EPS growth. We believe there is plenty of room for Informa to continue to take share in this highly fragmented global market, with management’s shifting capital allocation priorities providing a kicker to shareholder returns. A more conservatively managed balance sheet, consistent share buyback activity, and a resumption of its growing dividend have been welcome developments as the firm emerges from the industrywide shock caused by the pandemic. We were able to purchase shares at a mid-teens earnings multiple, which we believe undervalues the through-cycle growth potential available at this high-quality franchise.

Thank you for your continued support of Fiduciary Management, Inc.

Footnotes

1 Estimated valuations are calculated utilizing iShares ETF constituent level data as index level proxies. Calculations are based on the estimated valuations by looking at the average discount to the weighted average valuations as of 9/30/2024 for the trailing 1-year Price-to-Earnings Ratio, Fiscal Year One Price-to-Earnings Ratio, Fiscal Year Two Price-to- Earnings Ratio, Price-to-Sales Ratio, and Price-to-Book Ratio. Valuations are adjusted based on criteria identified by FMI. For more information, please contact FMI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sangoma Technologies: A Turnaround Showing Solid Progress
data.description:
Investment thesis

Having a market leading product platform, Sangoma Technologies (NASDAQ:SANG) differentiates itself from its competitors by being a single-vendor for its customers, catering to all their cloud communication needs at an affordable price. Its offering is mainly aimed at the SMB market, which the company estimates to be a $33 billion opportunity. Following the change in its go-to-market strategy implemented last year, the company is prepared to move further upmarket to tackle the needs of slightly larger customers. The success of this approach is reflected in its share price, which has doubled so far this year. Despite this significant share price appreciation, I find the current valuation of 5.7 times FCF very attractive, especially compared to its peers. While the outlook for growth remains muted, I expect earnings growth will remain strong due to likely margin expansion from FY26 onwards. Consequently, I rate SANG stock a Buy.

Reviewing recent results following the business transformation

Before Charles Salameh became CEO of Sangoma in September 2023, the company faced significant challenges, such as declining revenue, falling margins and a heavy debt burden, which resulted in its share price falling more than 90% from its peak in early 2021. A key element of the turnaround strategy he implemented was a transformation of the company's go-to-market approach.

Sangoma's diverse product offerings in the cloud communication market, including hardware, Unified Communications as a Service (UCaaS), Contact Center as a Service (CCaaS), Communications Platform as a Service (CPaaS) and SIP Trunking were restructured into a unified Sangoma brand. This bundling of products from prior acquisitions prioritized sales of higher-margin services, which achieve gross margins near 70% and churn rates below 1%. This strategy also allowed the company to better leverage demand generation from its strategic sales partners, enabling Sangoma’s sales team to collaborate more effectively with these partners.

By bundling hardware, software, and managed services, Sangoma positioned itself to better serve larger clients, thus moving upmarket and allowing it to build a more stable base of recurring service revenue. Recognizing its low brand recognition among end customers due to reliance on marketing partners, management has also made concerted efforts to enhance brand awareness.

The results from these actions appear to be gradually bearing fruit, as shown in the results above. Revenue declined 4% year over year in the final quarter of FY24, mainly driven by lower hardware sales due to macroeconomic headwinds. Meanwhile, services revenue remained flat and accounted for 81% of total revenue. Adjusted EBITDA rose 2% to $11.1 million at a margin of 18.2%, benefiting from cost reduction initiatives taken by management earlier this year, which resulted in annual cost savings of $9 million. Furthermore, due to prudent inventory management, cash from operating activities in Q4 rose 8% year over year to $11.7 million, supporting the company's deleveraging process.

Looking ahead: What investors can expect

Entering FY25 in a more stable financial position helped by the strong profitability of the business, management expressed optimism with full-year guidance implying 3% organic growth at the midpoint. Given that Q1 guidance points to a 2% year-over-year decline, investors can expect sequential improvement in year-over-year growth for the remaining quarters as the benefits of key growth drivers begin to materialize.

As illustrated above, the key drivers for future growth are organic growth from its existing products, expanding its offerings as well as its target market along with a strategic approach to potential M&A opportunities. Though it is difficult to estimate the potential from M&A, I remain confident in the ability for the company to grow from cross-selling its offerings to its existing customer base. Given the already low churn of less than 1%, this should lift net retention rates well above 100%, which when coupled with new sales, should support mid-single digit overall growth rates in the medium term. Furthermore, the potential for securing larger deals as the company moves further upmarket remains significant. Beginning in Q2, management plans to share certain go-to-market metrics so that investors get a better view of the company's growth outlook. Though it was not specifically mentioned, I expect the metrics to be related to pipeline size, average deal size and win-rates.

Management has also undertaken significant investments in an ERP program aimed at improving the efficiency of the entire business, including improving productivity, quality of our service and customer support. Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $44 million for FY25 includes approximately $2 million in one-time ERP costs, which depress margins to 17%. Adjusting for this, margins would have been above 18%, which I believe represents a base above which margins should improve from FY26 onwards, given the operating leverage in the business.

With the balance sheet comprising $78 million in debt and $17 million in cash, management's primary focus remains deleveraging. In addition to lowering interest related costs, reducing debt also allows management further flexibility with regard to capital allocation for M&A and returning capital to shareholders.

SANG stock valuation

At the current share price of $6, Sangoma Technologies has a market cap of $200 million. With net debt of $61 million, its enterprise value stands at $261 million. Based on the midpoint of management's full year adjusted EBITDA guidance for FY25 of $44 million, shares are valued at an EV/adjusted multiple of 5.9. Considering my estimates for annual capital expenditures and interest payments of $4 million and $5 million respectively, I expect FCF to be around $35 million, assuming no changes in working capital. This implies a Price/FCF multiple valuation of 5.7.

I would point out that stock-based compensation expenses at close to $3 million annually, remain at reasonable levels. While revenue growth expectations are muted, earnings growth has significant potential in the coming year due to margin expansion. Given this context, I find the current valuation quite attractive, especially when compared to a peer like RingCentral (RNG), which I have previously covered. RingCentral, focusing more on the enterprise segment, is growing slightly faster at high single-digit rates but has a much higher Price/FCF multiple of 7.5, representing a 30% premium. If Sangoma can achieve consistent organic growth and gradually improve its margin profile, I believe the shares could see a significant re-rating, narrowing the gap to its peers.

Risks to consider

Competition

The cloud communication solutions market is highly competitive, with large players such as Cisco (CSCO), 8x8 (EGHT), Microsoft (MSFT) Teams, Zoom (ZM), RingCentral as well as smaller players such as Crexendo (CXDO) and IDT (IDT) owned net2phone. Therefore, competition poses a risk to Sangoma's market position and growth potential. While the larger competitors primarily target the enterprise segment, Sangoma differentiates itself by focusing on the SMB segment and leveraging its ability to bundle essential hardware together with its wide range of services.

Balance Sheet Challenges

I consider the company's net debt level to be quite manageable, with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 1.4, particularly in light of ongoing deleveraging efforts. However, given management's ambitions for M&A, there is limited capacity to take on additional leverage without compromising the strength of the balance sheet.

Dependency on partners for sales and marketing

Unlike most of its competitors that have direct sales teams, Sangoma relies on partners for its sales and marketing efforts. While this approach offers certain advantages, it also limits direct engagement with end customers, which could result in weaker brand loyalty and slower feedback loops. So far, this risk has been minimal, as indicated by the low churn rates. However, investors should pay close attention to management's commentary on partner relationships and their effectiveness in driving success.

Conclusion

I view investing in SANG stock as an appealing opportunity, particularly due to the improved outlook following the company's initial successes in transforming its go-to-market strategy. Although challenges remain in achieving consistent organic growth and stronger profitability, especially in a competitive landscape, I believe the attractive valuation offers a solid margin of safety for investors.

data.title: Funko: Turnaround Measures Are Beginning To Bear Fruit (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Though the bank earnings season has all but declared a “soft landing” for the U.S. economy, lifting the S&P 500 to fresh heights, many pockets of the economy remain strapped by weaker consumer spending. But yet, amid this backdrop, pop-culture toy manufacturer Funko (NASDAQ:FNKO) has achieved a remarkable turnaround.

Year to date, under its new CEO Cynthia Williams, a former executive of much larger toymaker Hasbro (HAS), shares of Funko have jumped more than 50% on strong results (or at least, better than feared).

I last wrote a sell opinion on Funko in December, when the stock was trading under $7 per share. I’ll acknowledge that my sell call was premature and discounted the possibility of Funko successfully turning its business around. As a reminder, late last year the company set out its intentions to trim its workforce, change its executive leadership team, and perhaps most significantly, slice down the number of SKUs in its portfolio as it decided to shrink its inventory requirements and focus on its best-performing brands. So far, this strategy appears to be yielding fruit, prompting me to raise my outlook on Funko to neutral.

The chart below gives a great summary of the changes the company is putting in place:

Early into its turnaround plan, and at current share prices near $12, I have a more balanced view of Funko’s risk-reward profile going forward. On the bright side for the company:

  • Succeeding as a smaller portfolio. The company’s decision to shrink its SKU counts has had the desired effect of reducing inventory requirements, helping the company to also trim down its debt load for working capital. In addition, the company also reached a ~$2 million settlement with a recent shareholder lawsuit (that argued Funko misrepresented the value of its unsold inventory), removing an unwanted distraction from the stock and hopefully also addressing the core issue by producing faster-moving inventory itself.
  • Achieving growth again. And despite the portfolio shrinkage as well as U.S. macro headwinds, the company is growing revenue in the single digits, thanks to outsized growth internationally: indicating Funko’s potential in expanding overseas as it strikes more deals with international resellers.

Still, we have to be mindful of a number of risks here, which include:

  • Funko is heavily exposed to fads. Toy sales pick up around certain brands when there’s a catalyst (a film release or a new season of a popular TV show, for example). Funko’s portfolio reduction also means it is more reliant on its biggest hitters to deliver strong sales around these key sales moments.
  • Debt load. Funko is carrying a meaningful amount of net debt and has minimal cash, which is risky for a company that often flips between being profitable and sustaining losses.
  • New leadership team. There’s a new CEO and CFO in the C-suite. While so far the change-up has yielded positive results, the new team is still relatively untested.

All in all, I think Funko’s YTD pop has fairly compensated the company for the beginnings of a successful turnaround, but much remains to be done. I’d advocate remaining on the sidelines here, as I don’t think there’s much incentive from either a fundamental or valuation incentive to buy a fresh position at ~$13.

Q2 recap

Let’s now go through Funko’s latest quarterly results in greater detail to demonstrate where the company has surprised investors to the upside. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Funko’s revenue grew 3% y/y to $247.7 million, decimating Wall Street’s much more conservative estimate of $231.0 million, which would have represented a -4% y/y decline.

The charts below show detail on the company’s sales by segment. Clearly, de-assortment in the core collectibles category isn’t impeding growth in the category, up 6% y/y, though its acquired subsidiary Loungefly - maker of branded accessories like backpacks - is struggling. Still, we applaud the company’s 20%+ growth in Europe and Asia, though investors need to be mindful of the contraction in the U.S., which is more than two-thirds of overall revenue.

One word of caution: the company noted that $9 million of revenue in the quarter (worth roughly four points of growth) was due to reseller customers pulling forward shipments due to logistics concerns. This means that Q2 benefited from an unnatural channel inventory build that will unwind next quarter. Without this favorable shipment timing, revenue would have been only about flat y/y. However, the company also points out that these results are admirable amid the recent SKU reduction as well as the lingering impacts of a Hollywood strike that have resulted in a dearth of new content production (Funko benefits greatly from key sales moments when new movies and films are released). Per CFO Yves LePendeven’s remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

For the second quarter, total net sales were $247.7 million. Direct-to-consumer sales mix in Q2 was 23% of our gross sales, up from 18% in last year's Q2. This represents 33% direct-to-consumer sales growth, which we achieved despite a lack of new entertainment releases due primarily to the Hollywood strikes.

Our Q2 sales included a pull forward of approximately $9 million as certain customers placed orders earlier than usual in part to secure vessel space due to rising freight costs and container availability issues. The bottom line impact was approximately $2.5 million.”

We note as well that Funko’s gross margin rose sharply to 42%, 13 points better y/y. The company benefited both from lower inventory reserves (as in, losses from unsalable inventory that impacts gross margin, a direct result of the company’s decision to terminate product lines with poor sales) as well as favorable pricing on channel sales (though we do have to keep in mind the pull forward of shipments that benefited this quarter’s revenue).

Adjusted EBITDA also hit $27.9 million, or an 11% margin: 14 points better y/y, benefiting from the company’s workforce reductions on top of the margin gains (SG&A costs were 9% lower y/y in Q2, despite the ~flat revenue growth when accounting for the pull-forward of reseller shipments).

Q3 earnings preview

Funko is set to report Q3 earnings in early November. In my view, the key focus areas for investors to watch in the upcoming earnings cycle will be:

  • U.S. contraction: does the revenue decline in the U.S. get substantially worse, and what will the company say about partners' buying behavior amid macroeconomic concerns?
  • Gross margins: can the company keep up the same pace of y/y gross margin expansion as in Q3?

Funko has stated that one of its core near-term goals is to expand its partner distribution network. In my view, we're likely to see revenue eclipse results as the company aggressively pushes shipments onto its partners, but we might see some slight softening of gross margins in light of heavier trade discounts. All in all, I view the upcoming earnings to be a neutral catalyst for Funko, with a mixed bag of both positives and negatives that investors will pick up on.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $12, Funko trades at a market cap of $648.4 million. And after we net off the $41.6 million of cash and $223.9 million of total debt on its recent balance sheet (including $90 million drawn on its line of credit for working capital, which is down roughly 25% y/y thanks to the company’s inventory shrinkage), the company’s resulting enterprise value is $830.7 million.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year, Funko is guiding to $65-$85 million in adjusted EBITDA, or a ~7% EBITDA margin. At the midpoint of the guidance range, this puts the stock’s valuation at 11.0x EV/FY24 adjusted EBITDA.

Altogether, that’s not a terrible valuation, especially as Funko still has room to deliver upside on EBITDA margins this year and next if it can continue to deliver sales growth on its reduced SG&A cost base. But still, that multiple sits against a variety of risks, including sales declines in the U.S. amid a potential macro downturn, the risk of fading fads, and a new management team that may not have the experience to turn Funko around in a crisis.

At best, I’d say Funko’s YTD rally leaves the stock fairly valued. Keep this stock on your watch list, but don’t rush to buy this name on its recent momentum.

data.title: Capital Southwest: High Quality And Stable 10% Yield At The Right Time
data.description:
I have been covering Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC) since March this year, when I issued my first bullish article on this mid-sized BDC. Even though CSWC carries a NAV base of just around $770 million, it is arguably one of the highest-quality BDCs that investors can get.

My initial thesis here was predicated on CSWC's high-quality asset base, low leverage, consistent focus on non-speculative businesses, and a strongly covered dividend.

Since the publication of my initial article, the BDC has managed to outperform the index by a notable margin.

Theoretically, this might cause a concern for investors on whether CSWC has already exhausted its upside potential, and thus should be de-emphasized in portfolios.

Yet, looking at the most recent earnings results and, importantly, the underlying fundamentals that offer timely and well-needed protection in the context of the current BDC market headwinds, I would argue that CSWC should remain as a core holding in investor BDC portfolios.

Let me explain.

Thesis Review

In Q1, 2025 (which covers Q2, 2024 calendar period), CSWC kept registering solid performance, with the pretax net investment income landing at $0.69 per share, as compared to $0.68 per share in the previous quarter. This fact alone is noteworthy as there were not too many BDCs, which managed to expand their net investment income levels in Q2, 2024 relative to Q1, 2024.

There were several drivers behind this, but, in a nutshell, these were the key ones:

  • No headwinds from the non-accrual side.
  • Slow and steady portfolio (or asset) growth.
  • Accretive equity share issuance, which allows to fund new investments with a direct positive contribution to the NII generation.

In fact, as CSWC was one of the few BDCs that actually captured NII per share growth, it also was one of the few ones that announced an increase in its base dividend. Namely, the Board of Directors approved a regular/base dividend of $0.58 per share, which implies an uptick of $0.01 per share compared to the previous distribution level. On top of this, the supplemental dividend remained in force, providing a nice boost for the total yield through an additional $0.06 per share distributions. Currently, the total yield stands at ~ 10% and is underpinned by a healthy base dividend coverage of ~ 122%.

Now, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will identify some very positive dynamics, which bode well for the future earnings results as well (which will be announced on 11/1/2024).

CSWC's new investment activity came in at $108.1 million, which was more than enough to offset the organic paydowns of $77.2 million. This is very important to note because of two things:

  1. Not all BDCs have registered positive growth statistics as they struggle to source in sufficient deal volumes that could at least compensate the loan repayment activity.
  2. This fact alone should provide a meaningful stimulus for Q2, 2025 NII results, or at least come in handy for combating the pressures from the sector-wide spread compression.

Furthermore, CSWC has not sacrificed its strict underwriting policy standards just to keep the growth momentum alive. For example, these new transactions have been funded at loan-to-value levels of 40% to 50% and an overall leverage level of 3.5x (on a debt-to-EBITDA basis). In fact, this is even healthier than what currently CSWC has in its total portfolio, where the weighted average leverage stands at 3.8x. Plus, all of these transactions were made through first-lien senior secured loans (i.e., the safest capital structure position, which could be obtained by BDCs).

Speaking of quality, another important aspect, which is worth factoring in is the quarter-to-quarter weighted average EBITDA growth, which has once again resulted in a positive figure (this time at 4%).

Just as its peer Main Street Capital (MAIN), which trades at a premium to NAV, CSWC has also used its attractive equity finance option by raising around $38 million in Q1, 2025. Since it trades at a premium, these kinds of issuances remain accretive for the existing shareholders, and, most importantly, allow CSWC to maintain a very healthy leverage profile, without adding pressures on the dividend coverage front.

Last but not least, I would like to underscore the embedded equity advantage in CSWC's portfolio as, in my opinion, it could lead to a notable incremental value creation once the M&A markets open up. Namely, as of the end of Q1, 2025, CSWC had active 69 equity investments with a total fair value of $133 million, accounting for around 9% of the total portfolio value. The embedded unrealized appreciation thus amounts to $0.72 per share.

The Bottom Line

As we saw from the data points above, CSWC could be easily placed among the highest quality BDCs out there, such as MAIN, Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX), and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL).

What is interesting about CSWC is that it not only offers a robust dividend protection but also an optionality for delivering material upside from its ~ 9% equity investment stakes, which could be monetized, when the M&A markets come back in vogue.

Obviously, the risks from structural spread compression and still relatively depressed M&A activity are there, but I would not be that concerned about them given the significant margin of safety on the dividend and portfolio credit statistic end. Plus, the fact that CSWC has maintained consistent positive net investment levels, they should help offset further pressures from decreasing yield, which are likely to also occur in the upcoming earnings report on 11/1/2024.

In a nutshell, I would recommend having (or buying) Capital Southwest and treating it as a core BDC holding.

data.title: Selling Norfolk Southern Corporation
data.description:
It’s been a little over a year since I changed my stance on Norfolk Southern Corporation (NYSE:NSC) and turned bullish. Since then, the shares have returned about 38.3% against a gain of about 36.5% for the S&P 500, which is gratifying enough. The company has reported earnings since, obviously, so I want to review those to see if it makes sense to buy more, hold, or take profits on this stock and move the capital elsewhere. I’ll make this determination by looking at the health of the financial results, and the valuation, especially on a relative basis. Before getting into it, I want to spend some time sermonizing, so I’m going to sermonize. In my view, successful investing requires a willingness to go against the crowd. When the crowd is overly pessimistic about a company, you want to buy. At the same time, though, when the crowd gets too sanguine about a given company and drives a stock to lofty heights, you need to be willing to sell.

Those who read me regularly know that I offer a thesis statement near the beginning of each of my articles. This offers more than you might glean from the bullet points above, but less than you’d get from the entire article. The thesis statement allows you to understand my thinking up front so you can avoid the terrible humour and proper spelling that you might find in one of my typical articles. You’re welcome. I’ll be selling my Norfolk Southern shares. I think the valuation is now excessive, especially in light of the fact that the company is less profitable now than it was prior to the pandemic. The combination of decade high valuations and drastically lower profitability is never a good sign in my view, so I’m taking my winnings and leaving.

Contractual Obligations

Before getting into the heart of the analysis, I think it’s worthwhile pointing out what is in store for the company over the next few years. I think the technical term is “spicy meatball”, per the chart below. This year, the company is on the hook for about $3.25 billion, and an average of $1.7 billion in each of the next two years. The company currently has about $659 million in cash, so they’re going to need to tap capital or debt markets for some portion of these obligations. I don’t know if you’ve been keeping track, but interest rates are rather elevated at the moment, so there is risk that the company will be rolling debt at higher rates of interest. I think this provides an interesting backdrop for the remainder of the analysis.

Financial Snapshot

The first six months of the year haven’t been great in my estimation. Relative to the same period last year, revenue and net income are lower by 1% and 3.9% respectively. Additionally, the capital structure has deteriorated materially, with long term debt up by a whopping $2.193 billion, or 14.6%. In case you forgot about how bad 2023 was, I will remind you. In that year, net income was about 44% lower than it was in 2022. Thus, it’s not like the company is being benchmarked against a banner year. While on the topic of 2022, I would note that net income for the first six months of this year is lower than the same period in 2022 by $732 million, or 48%. If you’re itching to know how the most recent six-month period did relative to the same period prior to the pandemic, get ready to have that particular itch scratched. Net income for the first six months of 2024 was $609 million lower than it was the same period in 2019.

Given the above, I’m only willing to add to the position at the right price. If the valuation is too rich, I’m going to sell the stock.

The Stock

The phrase “at the right price” is very important in my view, because even a great, profitable company with a great “moat” can be a terrible investment if you overpay for it. Similarly, a company that is less profitable today than it was five years ago, such as this one, may be a great investment if you can pick it up at a decent price. The idea of buying cheap sounds great, but it’s a bit amorphous. I need to be a bit more concrete.

I apply a few metrics to decide if a stock is trading cheaply enough or not. Some of these are more complex than others. On the simple side, I look at the relationship between price and some measure of economic value like sales, or earnings, and the like. The lower the price per unit of economic value, the better. In particular, I want to see shares trading at a discount both to the overall market and to their own history. On this score, I think it's fair to say that the current valuation of Norfolk Southern is rather high. Specifically, the market is paying $4.79 for $1 of sales for this business that is less profitable now than it was in 2019. Per the following, when the market has reached these levels in the past, the shares have subsequently dropped in price. Additionally, on a PE basis, the stock is trading near a decade high. This is very troublesome in my view.

Source: YCharts

Source: YCharts

In addition to looking at simple ratios, I want to try to understand what the market is currently "thinking" about a given company's future. In order to do this, I turn to the work of Stephen Penman, particularly his book "Accounting for Value." One of the most interesting ideas expressed in this book is that share price itself is a valuable source of information, and gives us clues about what the market view of the future of the business is. So, if the market’s assumptions are too optimistic, that’s bad.

We get at the assumptions by applying some high school algebra to work out the “g” (growth) variable in a standard finance formula. The greater the expectations, the more risky the investment. According to this approach, the market currently "thinks" that Norfolk Southern will grow at a rate of about 6.6% from current levels. In my view, that is excessive for a company that is less profitable today than it was in 2019. This is yet another reason to avoid this stock at current levels.

data.title: Citigroup: I Am Buying Every Dip
data.description:
Citigroup (NYSE:C) delivered strong earnings in Q3-2024 which I will discuss later in this article. However, the stock fell 5% on the day partly due to profit-taking (the stock outperformed in the last 12 months) but mostly due to (unwarranted) concerns about fears of additional regulatory outreach (asset cap). I will also discuss why I believe the concern of further regulatory intrusion is unlikely. Still, first I would like to set out the opportunity and how that should feed into the valuation of the stock.

Forward Valuation of Citi Stock

Citigroup is currently trading at ~0.7x Tangible Book Value (TBV). The current TBV is ~$90; by the end of 2025, it will likely be ~$100 and ~$110 by the end of 2026. Citi's management is committed to RoTCE of ~11% to 12% by 2026. If we assume Citi hits its numbers, the stock should be valued somewhere in the range of 1.1x to 1.5x TBV or somewhere in the range of $120 to $170.

The above valuation naturally depends on Citi meeting its RoTCE target and at the high end of the valuation range also reflects (i) expectation of further improvement in returns beyond 2026 (ii) recognition that the quality of the Citi business model is much improved with stable, capital-light businesses such as Wealth and Services becoming a bigger part of the firm.

There is also additional potential upside from outsized share buybacks as capital is released from the Mexico IPO, finalization of the Basel III end-game capital rules, and lower capital requirements for Citi attributed to its much-simplified business model.

Of course, the Street is skeptical that Citi will meet its targets. Analysts believe that Citi will come short on Revenue (4% to 5% CAGR) and Expenses as they may need to spend more on regulatory fixes.

Even if we assume that Citi falls short of the returns target and only delivers 10% RoTCE by 2026 then the market may only reward Citi with a 0.8x TBV valuation. The share price by 2026, should be approximately $88. So in short, at a share price of $64 and a safe dividend of ~3%, there is a sufficient margin of safety in the current share price and upside optionality.

The key downside risks are macroeconomic and/or geopolitical shocks. The mitigating factor is that Citi, like all of the large U.S. banks, has effectively been derisked by over 15 years of regulatory reform that resulted in lower balance sheet risks and much higher capital and liquidity buffers. The other risk factor is regulatory outreach and intrusion.

There Is No Asset Cap

Mike Mayo, incidentally having Citigroup as his top pick, asked a question during the earnings call that caused the share price to dip as well as caught the management team completely unprepared. The question was as per below:

Mike Mayo

And I guess I'd highlight, as you know, on October 2nd, Senator Warren asked the OCC to impose growth restrictions because Citi is “too big to manage”. Now I would assume you don't agree with that, but still the question that a lot of people have is, what assurances can you give that an asset cap won't happen at Citigroup? Thanks

It seems that Citi's CEO was completely unprepared for this question and instead of simply immediately responding "There is no asset cap and we do not expect one", she provided a long-winded answer about the transformation and that she is:

very confident about the strategy we've laid out for the firm, the deliberate path we're on, the huge progress we've already made, and that we will continue making with determination and with clarity going forward.

Mr. Market interpreted this as a real risk and that Mike Mayo must know something that the market is unaware of. At that point, the stock dipped 5% and did not recover for the day.

Only much later on the call, Jane Fraser addressed the point directly:

So let me be crystal clear. We do not have an asset cap and there are no additional measures other than what was announced in July in place and not expecting any.

The Regulatory Concerns Are Over-Blown

Some context is important here. For unknown reasons, Mike Mayo brought up the asset cap as a potential risk presumably as it was applied in the Wells Fargo misconduct and misselling issues.

This is a completely different matter to delays in one of the workstreams dealing with Citi's consent order. It is mostly a technology and process issue and not customer or misconduct-related. It is simply ensuring complete and accurate data integrity feeding into regulatory reports the Group produces. Whilst it is undoubtedly a serious matter, it is resolvable within a relatively short time and more importantly an asset cap remedy could not feasibly be on the cards for such an issue.

Strong Q3-2024 Performance

Citi delivered a strong set of results with positive operating leverage across all divisions.

  • Services revenue increased by 8% year on year fueled by fee income growth and a stellar performance from Security Services with 24% year-over-year revenue growth. Services delivered an exceptional 26.4% RoTCE for Q2-2024
  • Markets benefited from a strong performance from Prime Finance in equities (recurring income) somewhat offset by lower income in FICC (compared with a very strong prior year comparison). It still managed to deliver 1% revenue growth year on year whereas expenses decreased by 1% year on year.
  • Banking delivered a very strong performance with Investment Banking fees up 44% driven by DCM (+75%). This has been driven by capital markets recovery but also by Citi gaining market share.
  • Wealth delivered 9% revenue growth year on year whereas expenses declined by 4% as management right-sized its operations and delivered strong operating leverage.
  • US Personal Banking ("USPB") also delivered operating leverage including 3% revenue growth year on year and a 1% decrease in expenses.

The full-year 2024 guidance also confirmed that Citi is expected to meet both Revenue (expected at the higher end) and Expense targets.

Final Thoughts

Mr. Market is not giving Citi much credit despite clear progress and is highly skeptical of Citi's ability to deliver on the transformations and 2026 targets. I see 2025 as being the inflection year where doubters may just become believers. The upside is very significant coupled with a high margin of safety, I see the investment case as asymmetric.

The catalysts are multiple and include increased share buybacks, continued revenue growth, and progress on resolving the consent orders. At 0.7x TBV, I remain very bullish and continue to accumulate on dips.

data.title: TSMC Defies Market Concerns With Q3 Earnings Beat
data.description:
Investment Thesis

TSMC (NYSE:TSM) has rallied 13% since our last coverage, narrowing the gap toward our target price of $210. Following its Q3 2024 earnings, which showed substantial year-over-year (YoY) revenue and profit growth, we believe TSMC’s long-term prospects remain intact. The company’s rapid growth is a testament to its success in fulfilling tech giants’ expanding demands. Advanced technologies like 3-nm and impending 2-nm chips place TSMC in a better position to satisfy this growing demand.

Its strategic focus on AI and high-performance computing (HPC) keeps us bullish on the company growing fast and reaching higher margins than traditional segments. Despite the high CapEx being a concern, it’s necessary to equip TSMC as the leading supplier of advanced chips. Innovation and a dominant market share have been the pillars of TSMC’s success, ensuring steady future revenue and profitable growth.

TSMC Delivers Strong Q3 2024: Powered by AI and HPC Demand, Revenues Surge 39% YoY

TSMC had a strong Q3 of 2024, posting revenues of NT$759.69 billion, approximately $23.5 billion, up 39% YoY and 12.8% quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) compared to Q2/2024. The company reported Q3 GAAP EPS of $1.94, beating estimates by $0.15, with revenue of $23.5 billion exceeding expectations by $210 million. Net incomes during the same period were up 54.2% YoY at NT$325.26 billion, reflecting the company’s ability to capitalize on highly advanced chips in AI and HPC applications.

Not surprisingly, Q3 revenue has exceeded the analyst consensus forecast of ~NT$748 billion (~$23.21 billion), signaling that TSMC’s revenue generation capacity exceeds the street standards based on its AI-technology-related chips. Against Q2 2024 (NT$673.51 billion), the sequential quarterly growth of 12.8% marks the robustness of AI-driven demand in propelling the company’s top-line growth.

Moreover, the key driver for TSMC’s substantial profitability growth is a net income increase of 31.2% QoQ and an underlined operational efficiency to scale up production to meet increasing demand. Strategic focus on high-margin sectors like AI, HPC, and 5G continues to drive demand for advanced chips and help TSMC achieve more substantial margins and revenue growth.

Remarkably, September 2024’s YoY revenue increased by 39.6%, directly corresponding with the increasing demand for AI chips. Driven by the need for HPC, TSMC is considered a lead supplier of critical technology to tech giants like Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), and AMD (AMD) under AI adoption and may continue to support its top-line growth.

Most importantly, though, the strong position of TSMC in leading-edge processes, the 3nm process in particular, is a very real and tangible bridging point between these revenues at the very top and ongoing advancements in tech. For Q2 2024, the 3nm technology accounted for 15% of wafer revenue from 6% in Q3 2023. This growth reflects the market’s increasing reliance on smaller, more efficient chips for AI, smartphones, and HPC applications. For instance, 59% of the company’s total Q3 2023 revenue stemmed from advanced technologies (7nm and below). Now, this share expanded to 67% by Q2 2024.

In Q3 2024, the 3nm, 5nm, and 7nm technologies together accounted for 69% of the total wafer revenue, compared to 67% in Q2 2024. This means that TSMC still leads the process race in semiconductor manufacturing, while expanding its share in the rapidly growing AI and HPC markets.

Besides, the move to value chains of advanced technologies underpins TSMC’s ability to maintain its robust margins, with the gross margin reaching 57.8% in Q3 2024. Continuing revenue growth and a steady cadence of adoptions at advanced nodes testify to the company’s leadership position in the semiconductor space and ability to meet future demand for a high-performance chip as markets evolve.

TSMC also claims that production of its 2nm process technology is targeted to begin in full-volume production in 2025. The N2 can provide a 10-15% speed improvement at the same power or a 25-30% power improvement at the same speed. With that, there is a 15% chip density increase over its 3nm predecessor.

The new tape-outs for 2nm in the first two years may outpace both the 3nm and 5nm technologies in their early production phases. This aggressive ramp-up of N2 technology mirrors the strong demand already seen in N3. Rapid adoption rates are fundamental to a secure pipeline of clients (like Nvidia and AMD) eager to adopt these energy-efficient solutions. However, these factors may not be integrated into the company’s consensus revenue growth estimates for 2025 and 2026.

Furthermore, the company’s extension of N2 technology with N2P provides an additional 5% performance or 5% to 10% power efficiency gains. N2P is slated for production in H2 2026. These simultaneous enhancements provide TSMC with a solid competitive advantage in performance and efficiency.

Moreover, the N2P technology supports both smartphone and HPC applications that may serve the dual demands of consumer electronics and enterprise computing. For instance, HPC contributed 42% of total revenues in Q3 2023. By Q2, 2024, this share had risen significantly to 52% as more industries increasingly require advanced computing capabilities (particularly in AI, data analytics, and machine learning).

In contrast, smartphone revenue, traditionally one of TSMC’s core segments, has declined from 39% in Q3 2023 to 33% in Q2 2024. While this decline may appear concerning, it points to a strategic pivot towards HPC and AI. Here, margins are typically higher, and long-term growth potential is greater. Hence, this strategic shift justifies the focus on developing 2nm and 3nm chips.

In other words, the global AI market will be over $638 billion in 2024 and may reach ~3.7 trillion by 2034 at a CAGR of 19.1% (2024-2034). On the other hand, revenues across the US AI market stood at 123.07 billion in 2023 and may surpass ~851 billion by 2034, growing at an annual rate of 19.3% (2024-2034).

Finally, TSMC’s top line protects against regional economic issues. For instance, North America remains TSMC’s largest market and consistently contributes a major portion of revenue. The region accounted for 69% of the revenue in Q3 2023 and 65% in Q2 2024. Similarly, China’s revenue share increased from 9% in Q1 2024 to 16% in Q2, reflecting the expanding demand for semiconductors in this region despite US-China tensions.

Massive CapEx and Cash Flow Strain

A structural weakness in TSMC’s growth potential derives from its massive CapEx that considerably strains its cash flow and financials. Considering the quarterly trends from Q3 2023 through Q3 2024, CapEx constantly exceeded NT$170 billion per quarter. In Q3 2023, CapEx was NT$226.62 billion, dropping to NT$170.16 billion in Q4 2023. Then, it rose to NT$181.3 billion, NT$205.68 billion, and NT$207.08 in Q1, Q2, and Q3 2024, respectively.

These are massive capital outlays to maintain TSMC’s competitive edge as it invests in advanced node manufacturing processes (3nm and 2nm). However, such heavy investments can hinder short-term profitability and pressure cash flow. This may hurt stock valuation based on turns in the street sentiment if returns on these investments do not materialize as quickly as anticipated.

For instance, TSMC has experienced a gradual erosion in its ROIC that signals inefficiencies in capital deployment and challenges in maintaining return trends as its scale and CapEx requirements grow. TSMC’s ROIC in 2014 was 21.83%, and for a couple of years, it ranged evenly around ~21%; then, it slowly drifted downward and hit 19.86% by 2018, continually falling to 18.78% in 2019.

Although the company saw a resumption in its ROIC in 2020 (reaching 25.19%), this upward trend was temporary. By 2023, TSMC’s ROIC had dropped to 19.81%; in 2024, TTM’s ROIC was flat at 19.74%. The decline in ROIC suggests that the company is generating less profit from its capital investments than in previous years. This is attributed to the increasing complexity and cost of advanced semiconductors that may not yield proportionally high returns. This eroding profitability from capital investment directly impacts the stock valuations, as ongoing projects may struggle to justify the enormous capital outlays based on current ROIC trends.

Takeaway

TSMC’s strong revenue momentum, driven by AI and high-performance computing, supports the corporation’s long-term growth prospects. Demand from major clients and new chip technology developments place TSMC in an excellent position to maintain its competitive advantage in this market. Despite high capital expenditures, its strategic focus on AI and HPC ensures continued revenue and profit expansion.

data.title: Starbucks: Big Unionization Problem
data.description:
Co-Authored By Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) has been under a lot of pressure as the global coffee chain has been trying to buck a slowdown and bring in new management to set up a turnaround. The Seattle-based coffee giant is facing increasingly tough competition, and sales are slowing down. Recently, Starbucks brought in a new CEO (Brian Niccol from Chipotle (CMG)) to help kickstart the overall growth of the iconic coffee chain.

While Niccol has a lot of experience turning around major food chains, the biggest issue Starbucks faces at the moment isn’t even with its business model. Labor risk is becoming a huge problem for the company as more stores unionize.

Starbucks stores have been undergoing a mass wave of unionization during the last two years, with over 500 stores unionized already. The biggest impact of this is that the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) has become critical of Starbucks and how they operate their stores (more on this later). This is an enormous risk to their business because Starbucks is not in a high-margin business.

Unions are beginning to catch on to the automation trend that is spreading across food prep and manufacturing. To combat this, they’re also demanding in contract negotiations that their workforces and jobs not be automated. Starbucks depends on future automation growth to help fuel reinvestment back into the business. Missing this automation trend is a huge growth drag

To be clear, I think Brian Niccol is a great leader. But all leaders are imperfect and have their faults. For Brian, I don’t see how one of his strengths is unions (Chipotle stores are not unionized anywhere near the same level). Unions bring a unique challenge for Brian, and I don’t think the stock is pricing this in. With this, I think Starbucks shares are a sell. On a P/E basis, they’re expensive, and I think any upside will likely be capped by labor risk.

Background

Starbucks was founded in 1971 by Gordon Bowker, Zev Siegl and Jerry Baldwin. The three men and their vision for a different type of coffee shop was launched from their first location in Seattle, Washington. Throughout the ‘70s, Starbucks performed well, but accelerated in the 1980s.

In 1982, Howard Schultz got connected with the coffee brand and developed a passion for not only coffee, but the inviting, romantic atmosphere that allowed for people to connect and converse with each other over a cup of coffee. Schultz served as Starbucks’ CEO for a total of 23 years.

Over the last three years, as companies across the globe have worked to rebound from the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, Starbucks has had an increasingly difficult time. Newer and smaller niche coffee shops have sprouted up, resulting in Starbucks losing out on its target audience and losing its spot in many cities as the preferred location for people to show up, connect and converse.

Niccol, though, is supposed to be the answer Starbucks bulls are looking for. The newly named CEO has played a critical role in the turnarounds of Chipotle and Taco Bell, and he has the opportunity to do the same thing with Starbucks.

Niccol spent seven years at Taco Bell, and he joined the company during a time in which sales had stalled significantly. Following his seven-year stint with Taco Bell, Niccol became the CEO of Chipotle, where he helped double revenue, increase profits by almost seven times and increase the stock price of the company by nearly eight times.

The big difference between these turnaround stories and what Niccol is facing today is labor risk. And I think this is a big one.

According to a Reuters article from June, Starbucks had roughly 400 locations throughout the United States that had unionized, which involved more than 10,000 employees. The unionization efforts are clearly picking up steam, though, with the number of unionized locations now topping 500 stores.

Why Unions Are A Unique Problem For Brian Niccol

Unionization is a unique problem for Niccol, and it’s something that he has not had to deal with in the past. As of August, Chipotle had only one unionized store in the entire United States.

Taco Bell, the other restaurant Niccol was CEO of, is also completely incomparable to Starbucks as it relates to a unionized workforce.

This issue is something Niccol has not had to work through in the past, and it could provide him and Starbucks with a unique roadblock in the future.

During his time at Chipotle, one of the things Niccol pushed for the hardest was increased efficiency gains both in portion sizes and ingredient usage to improve margins. I wrote previously about these concerns with Chipotle in the spring before Niccol left the company.

Now with increasing unionization pressure at Starbucks, Niccol faces a key challenge.

The proportion of Starbucks locations that are unionized is still very small, but it's growing fast. Of the reported 16,482 locations in the United States, only just over 500 are unionized. That is just over 3% of locations that are unionized. But this means the majority of the labor risk from these unionizations is ahead of the coffee chain, not behind it. The probability that workers at these locations push back on the efficiency gains that Niccol will likely go for is fairly high.

Valuation

As the coffee chain’s CEO was replaced, Starbucks has seen earnings estimates largely move downward over the last three months as Wall Street analysts begin to price in the fact that a turnaround will likely be painful.

I think with this, the market has also started to price in rising labor costs, which will be a big deal for margins.

Currently, Starbucks sports a gross profit margin of 27.61%, which is higher than their 5-year average of 26.32%. Their net income margin of 11.16% is also higher than their 5-year average of 10.39% and significantly higher than the sector median of 4.64%.

In essence, they are likely over earning right now. My fear is that unions driving wages higher will make these metrics revert to the mean. This means lower EPS if revenue does not pick up. And with revenue estimates moving downward, we may not see the lift that’s needed.

Shares currently trade in the mid-20s forward P/E ratio. The forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 26.72 is significantly higher than the sector median number of 18.58. This doesn’t make sense to me, as I think margins face a real risk. I think the forward P/E should probably be closer to 20 to reflect the labor risks the coffee giant faces. If the P/E moved closer to 20, which would represent 25.14% downside.

Bull Thesis

In my opinion, the biggest bull thesis for Starbucks is that the NLRB was struck down in June by the US Supreme Court for overstepping some of their rulings. The NLRB was trying to take action against Starbucks for firing workers as they sought to unionize. The upside from this ruling is that bulls could argue this is a sign that the unionization drive reached a dead end.

From the June court ruling, the Supreme Court sided with Starbucks, ruling that Starbucks does not have to reinstate its workers while the administrative case continues.

The justices unanimously threw out a lower court's approval of an injunction sought by the U.S. National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) ordering Starbucks to reinstate the workers while the agency's in-house administrative case against the Seattle-based company proceeds.

The Reuters article also revealed that the NLRB has received hundreds of complaints accusing Starbucks of “unlawful labor practices such as firing union supporters, spying on workers and closing stores during labor campaigns.”

Starbucks, though, has denied all of these claims. It’s this second line about hundreds of complaints that I find concerning. Because while Starbucks may or may not end up being found guilty for firing workers who attempted to unionize, the coffee chain would be in much bigger trouble if there has been a larger, orchestrated attempt underway to limit unionization. This is illegal in the United States.

I do not think this Supreme Court ruling will be enough to stop the unionization. Most unionization activities by Starbucks employees are fairly lawful and legitimate. In fact, just earlier this month, the Starbucks Worker's Union (SBWU) signed their 500th store to unionize. I don’t think this trend is going to stop anytime soon. With this, I think this continues to be a real risk to their business model.

Takeaway

The wave of unionization that is impacting Starbucks is unlike frankly anything we have seen at a fast food chain in the US. It's also a largely new and unique experience for their new CEO, Brian Niccol. Niccol has great experience turning around other fast food companies (Chipotle and Taco Bell), but neither of those two had a strong push to unionize like Starbucks currently is facing.

Unionization will continue to be a major risk to Starbucks business model and will not go away. I think this will push margins down in the near future. Shareholders will likely suffer.

At this point, I just don’t see what the growth drivers are to justify why the stock trades at such a high forward price-to-earnings ratio. On the surface, this higher price to earnings ratio could be justified by the new CEO. But Niccol faces these unique challenges. I believe his management technique is incongruent with what the labor force is set to do at Starbucks.

With this, I am a sell on shares of the coffee giant. If we saw a change in unionization trends at Starbucks, then I may be more likely to change my outlook.
